results.possibleIdentity: Dolores H. "Dilly" Schapiro (1930–2022) was an environmental designer, architectural professional, and arts patron active in New York City during the late 1960s and 1970s. Born in New York and a resident of Manhattan for much of her life, she earned a bachelor's degree in environmental design from the Parsons School of Design in 1973 and attended the Columbia University School of Architecture. During the 1970s, she worked for several New York architectural firms and served on the staff of the West Side Highway Project, a major urban and environmental initiative to upgrade Manhattan's shoreline. Schapiro was a prominent arts enthusiast and a long-time patron of major New York cultural institutions, including the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), the Whitney Museum of American Art, and the Metropolitan Museum of Art. Her background in environmental design and architecture in New York during the peak of experimental art-science collaborations aligns with the activities of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization.

Dolores Shapiro, PhD, RN, is a nurse and anthropologist who has contributed to the intersection of art and healthcare. She co-authored "The HeART of Nursing: Expressions of Creative Art in Nursing" (2nd edition, 2005), which explores creative and artistic expressions within the nursing profession. She received her doctoral degree in cultural anthropology from Columbia University and has taught at several institutions, including Columbia University and Rutgers. Her research has frequently focused on the cultural and symbolic aspects of health and religion, particularly in Brazil.
